annotations.label: Kinematics

anchor.snippet: Kinematics is a subfield of physics and a branch of geometry

annotations.note: A physics subfield, not a mathematical object that Mathlib formalizes.

annotations.label: Kinematics (classical mechanics)

anchor.snippet: without considering the forces that cause them to move

annotations.note: A physics discipline description with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relativistic kinematics

anchor.snippet: Relativistic kinematics applies the special theory of relativity to the geometry of object motion

annotations.note: Special-relativity physics; Mathlib has no relativistic-kinematics development.

annotations.label: Position of a particle

anchor.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchor.snippet: The position of a particle is defined as the coordinate vector from the origin of a coordinate frame to the particle

annotations.note: A position is just an element of a coordinate/Euclidean space, which Mathlib formalizes, but the physics notion itself is not named.

annotations.label: Tower position vector

anchor.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchor.snippet: consider a tower 50 m south from your home

annotations.note: A worked numerical example with no formal Mathlib analogue.

annotations.label: Position vector

anchor.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchor.snippet: The position vector of a particle is a vector drawn from the origin of the reference frame to the particle

annotations.note: The underlying object is a vector in Euclidean space, which Mathlib has, but the physics framing is not formalized.

anchors.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchors.snippet: The position vector of a particle is a vector drawn from the origin of the reference frame to the particle

annotations.label: Magnitude of position vector

anchor.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchor.snippet: gives the distance between the point

annotations.note: The magnitude equals the Euclidean norm/distance, given by `EuclideanSpace.norm_eq`, generalizing this special case.

anchors.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchors.snippet: gives the distance between the point

annotations.label: Trajectory of a particle

anchor.section: Kinematics of a particle trajectory in a non-rotating frame of reference
anchor.snippet: which defines the curve traced by the moving particle

annotations.note: A narrative notion of a traced curve; not a named Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Average velocity

anchor.snippet: This ratio is called the average velocity over that time interval and is defined as

annotations.note: A physics difference quotient over time with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

anchors.snippet: This ratio is called the average velocity over that time interval and is defined as

anchors.value: {\displaystyle \mathbf {\bar {v}} ={\frac {\Delta \mathbf {r} }{\Delta t}}={\frac {\Delta x}{\Delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+{\frac {\Delta y}{\Delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}+{\frac {\Delta z}{\Delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {z} }}={\bar {v}}_{x}{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+{\bar {v}}_{y}{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}+{\bar {v}}_{z}{\hat {\mathbf {z} }}\,}

annotations.label: Instantaneous velocity

anchor.snippet: the average velocity approaches the instantaneous velocity, defined as the time derivative of the position vector

annotations.note: Velocity is the (first) derivative of a vector-valued position function, an application of Mathlib's general derivative operators.

anchors.snippet: the average velocity approaches the instantaneous velocity, defined as the time derivative of the position vector

anchors.value: {\displaystyle \mathbf {\bar {v}} ={\frac {\Delta \mathbf {r} }{\Delta t}}={\frac {\Delta x}{\Delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+{\frac {\Delta y}{\Delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}+{\frac {\Delta z}{\Delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {z} }}={\bar {v}}_{x}{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+{\bar {v}}_{y}{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}+{\bar {v}}_{z}{\hat {\mathbf {z} }}\,}

annotations.label: Speed

anchor.snippet: The speed of an object is the magnitude of its velocity

annotations.note: Speed is the norm of the velocity vector, computed by Mathlib's Euclidean norm.

anchors.snippet: The speed of an object is the magnitude of its velocity

annotations.label: Average acceleration

anchor.snippet: The average acceleration of a particle over a time interval is defined as the ratio

annotations.note: A physics difference quotient of velocity with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

anchors.snippet: The average acceleration of a particle over a time interval is defined as the ratio

annotations.label: Instantaneous acceleration

anchor.snippet: The acceleration of the particle is the limit of the average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero

annotations.note: Acceleration is the derivative of velocity, an instance of Mathlib's general derivative machinery.

anchors.snippet: The acceleration of the particle is the limit of the average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero

annotations.label: Acceleration as derivatives of position

anchor.snippet: acceleration is the first derivative of the velocity vector and the second derivative of the position vector

annotations.note: The second derivative of a vector-valued function is `iteratedDeriv 2`, generalizing this statement.

annotations.label: Magnitude of acceleration

anchor.snippet: The magnitude of the acceleration of an object is the magnitude

annotations.note: The magnitude is the Euclidean norm of the acceleration vector.

anchors.snippet: The magnitude of the acceleration of an object is the magnitude

annotations.label: Relative position vector

anchor.snippet: A relative position vector is a vector that defines the position of one point relative to another

annotations.note: This is just a vector difference; the physics concept is not formalized as a named entity.

annotations.label: Relative velocity

anchor.snippet: The velocity of one point relative to another is simply the difference between their velocities

annotations.note: A difference of velocity vectors with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

anchors.snippet: The velocity of one point relative to another is simply the difference between their velocities

annotations.label: Relative acceleration

anchor.snippet: The acceleration of one point C relative to another point B is simply the difference between their accelerations

annotations.note: A difference of acceleration vectors with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

anchors.snippet: The acceleration of one point C relative to another point B is simply the difference between their accelerations

annotations.label: Velocity from integrating acceleration

anchor.snippet: the first integration yields the velocity of the particle as a function of time

annotations.note: Recovering velocity by integrating acceleration is an invocation of the fundamental theorem of calculus.

anchors.snippet: the first integration yields the velocity of the particle as a function of time

annotations.label: Constant-acceleration displacement equation

anchor.snippet: Additional relations between displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time can be derived

annotations.note: A specific constant-acceleration kinematics formula not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Additional relations between displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time can be derived

annotations.label: Velocity–position relation (time-independent)

anchor.snippet: A relationship between velocity, position and acceleration without explicit time dependence can be obtained

annotations.note: The closed-form v² = v₀² + 2a·d kinematics identity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Displacement from area under velocity–time graph

anchor.snippet: This equation is applicable when the final velocity v is unknown

annotations.note: Displacement as the integral (area) of velocity is an application of the fundamental theorem of calculus.

anchor.snippet: Consider a particle P that moves only on the surface of a circular cylinder

annotations.note: A specific physics trajectory setup with no named Mathlib formalization.

anchor.snippet: The velocity vector v P is the time derivative of the trajectory

annotations.note: The explicit cylindrical-coordinate velocity decomposition is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: the acceleration a P , which is the time derivative of the velocity

annotations.note: The explicit cylindrical-coordinate acceleration formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Centripetal acceleration

anchor.snippet: acts toward the center of curvature of the path at that point on the path

annotations.note: A physics acceleration component with no dedicated Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Coriolis acceleration

anchor.snippet: is called the Coriolis acceleration

annotations.note: A rotating-frame physics term with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Constant-radius velocity and acceleration

anchor.snippet: If the trajectory of the particle is constrained to lie on a cylinder, then the radius r is constant

annotations.note: A specialized constant-radius kinematics computation absent from Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: If the trajectory of the particle is constrained to lie on a cylinder, then the radius r is constant

annotations.label: Planar circular trajectory

anchor.snippet: A special case of a particle trajectory on a circular cylinder occurs when there is no movement along the z axis

annotations.note: A worked planar-motion example with no formal Mathlib analogue.

anchors.snippet: A special case of a particle trajectory on a circular cylinder occurs when there is no movement along the z axis

annotations.label: Radial and tangential acceleration components

anchor.snippet: are called, respectively, the radial and tangential components of acceleration

annotations.note: Physics decomposition of acceleration with no dedicated Mathlib definition.

anchors.snippet: are called, respectively, the radial and tangential components of acceleration

annotations.label: Geometry as study of invariants

anchor.section: Point trajectories in a body moving in the plane
anchor.snippet: Geometry is the study of the properties of figures that remain the same while the space is transformed in various ways

annotations.note: A philosophical characterization of geometry, not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Rigid transformations

anchor.section: Point trajectories in a body moving in the plane
anchor.snippet: Rigid transformations are those that preserve the distance between any two points

annotations.note: Distance-preserving maps are exactly Mathlib's `Isometry` (characterized by `isometry_iff_dist_eq`), generalized to arbitrary metric spaces.

annotations.label: Special Euclidean group SE(n)

anchor.section: Point trajectories in a body moving in the plane
anchor.snippet: is called the special Euclidean group on R n , and denoted SE( n )

annotations.note: The rotation part (`specialOrthogonalGroup`) and translations exist separately, but SE(n) is not assembled as a named group in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Displacement (rotation and translation)

anchor.snippet: A displacement consists of the combination of a rotation and a translation

annotations.note: Rotations and translations are affine equivalences whose composition Mathlib supports, but 'displacement' as such is not a named object.

annotations.label: Configuration space

anchor.snippet: is called the configuration space of M

annotations.note: The mechanics notion of configuration space is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Motion of a body

anchor.snippet: A smooth curve from one position to another in this configuration space is a continuous set of displacements

annotations.note: A smooth path through configuration space; no dedicated Mathlib formalization of body motion.

annotations.label: Homogeneous transform

anchor.snippet: can be represented by a certain type of 3×3 matrix known as a homogeneous transform

annotations.note: The specific homogeneous (3×3) matrix representation of planar motions is not a named Mathlib construction.

anchors.snippet: can be represented by a certain type of 3×3 matrix known as a homogeneous transform

annotations.label: Homogeneous transform of plane points

anchor.snippet: These homogeneous transforms perform rigid transformations on the points in the plane z = 1

annotations.note: The action of homogeneous transforms on the z=1 plane is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: These homogeneous transforms perform rigid transformations on the points in the plane z = 1

annotations.label: Homogeneous transforms are affine

anchor.snippet: Homogeneous transforms represent affine transformations

annotations.note: Affine transformations are formalized as `AffineMap`, but the homogeneous-transform representation linking them is not.

annotations.label: Pure translation

anchor.snippet: the motion is called pure translation

annotations.note: A translation is exactly Mathlib's `AffineEquiv.constVAdd`, a special case of affine maps.

anchors.snippet: the motion is called pure translation

annotations.label: Angular position

anchor.snippet: the oriented distance from a selected origin on the rotational axis to a point of an object

annotations.note: A physics angular-position notion with no dedicated Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Angular velocity

anchor.snippet: the angular velocity ω is the rate at which the angular position θ changes with respect to time

annotations.note: Angular velocity as a physics rate-of-change is not formalized as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Velocity of a point in 3D moving body

annotations.label: Acceleration of a point in 3D moving body

anchor.snippet: The acceleration of a point P in a moving body B is obtained as the time derivative of its velocity vector

annotations.note: This rigid-body point-acceleration formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kinematic constraints

anchor.snippet: Kinematic constraints are constraints on the movement of components of a mechanical system

annotations.note: A mechanical-engineering constraint notion with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Kinematic coupling

anchor.snippet: A kinematic coupling exactly constrains all 6 degrees of freedom

annotations.note: A mechanical-design concept not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rolling without slipping condition

anchor.snippet: An object that rolls against a surface without slipping obeys the condition that the velocity of its center of mass is equal to the cross product of its angular velocity

annotations.note: The rolling-constraint kinematics condition is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: An object that rolls against a surface without slipping obeys the condition that the velocity of its center of mass is equal to the cross product of its angular velocity

annotations.label: Inextensible cord constraint

anchor.snippet: The constraint is that the sum of lengths of all segments of the cord is the total length

annotations.note: A mechanical constraint condition not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kinematic pairs

anchor.snippet: Reuleaux called the ideal connections between components that form a machine kinematic pairs

annotations.note: A mechanism-theory concept with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Lower pair

anchor.snippet: A lower pair is an ideal joint, or holonomic constraint, that maintains contact between a point, line or plane in a moving solid

annotations.note: A mechanical-joint classification not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Revolute pair

anchor.snippet: A revolute pair, or hinged joint, requires a line, or axis, in the moving body to remain co-linear with a line in the fixed body

annotations.note: A specific mechanical joint type with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Prismatic joint

anchor.snippet: A prismatic joint , or slider, requires that a line, or axis, in the moving body remain co-linear with a line in the fixed body

annotations.note: A specific mechanical joint type with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Cylindrical joint

anchor.snippet: A cylindrical joint requires that a line, or axis, in the moving body remain co-linear with a line in the fixed body

annotations.note: A specific mechanical joint type with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Spherical joint

anchor.snippet: A spherical joint, or ball joint, requires that a point in the moving body maintain contact with a point in the fixed body

annotations.note: A specific mechanical joint type with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Planar joint

anchor.snippet: A planar joint requires that a plane in the moving body maintain contact with a plane in fixed body

annotations.note: A specific mechanical joint type with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Higher pair

anchor.snippet: a higher pair is a constraint that requires a curve or surface in the moving body to maintain contact with a curve or surface in the fixed body

annotations.note: A mechanism-theory joint classification not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cam joint

anchor.snippet: the contact between a cam and its follower is a higher pair called a cam joint

annotations.note: A mechanical example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Kinematic chains

anchor.snippet: are known as kinematic chains

annotations.note: A mechanism-assembly concept not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mobility formula for degrees of freedom

anchor.snippet: The degree of freedom of a kinematic chain is computed from the number of links and the number and type of joints using the mobility formula

annotations.note: The Chebychev–Grübler–Kutzbach mobility formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Planar one-DOF linkages

anchor.snippet: The planar one degree-of-freedom linkages assembled from N links and j hinges or sliding joints are

annotations.note: An enumeration of mechanical linkages with no Mathlib formalization.
